flashcards.front: What is the formal definition of polynomial time?
flashcards.back: An algorithm runs in polynomial time if its time complexity is O(n^k) for some constant k, where n is the input size. Examples include O(n), O(n²), O(n³), and O(n^100).

flashcards.front: What does the class P represent in complexity theory?
flashcards.back: P (Polynomial) is the class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time O(n^k). These are considered 'efficiently solvable' problems.

flashcards.front: What does NP stand for and what does it mean?
flashcards.back: NP (Non-deterministic Polynomial) is the class of problems where a solution can be verified in polynomial time, though finding the solution may not be polynomial.

flashcards.front: What is the P vs NP problem?
flashcards.back: The P vs NP problem asks whether every problem whose solution can be verified in polynomial time can also be solved in polynomial time. It remains an unsolved millennium prize problem.
A polynomial-time reduction is a transformation from problem A to problem B that can be computed in polynomial time, showing that if B is in P then A is also in P.
A problem is NP-complete if: (1) it is in NP, and (2) every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time.
SAT (Boolean Satisfiability) asks whether there exists an assignment of boolean variables that makes a given boolean formula true. It was the first problem proven NP-complete.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between tractable and intractable problems?
flashcards.back: Tractable problems are those solvable in polynomial time (in P). Intractable problems require super-polynomial time (like exponential time) and are generally considered impractical.

flashcards.front: Why is polynomial time used as the boundary for efficiency?
flashcards.back: Polynomial time provides a stable boundary because: (1) polynomials are closed under composition, (2) polynomial algorithms scale reasonably with input growth, and (3) it separates 'practical' from 'impractical' runtimes.

flashcards.front: What is an exponential time algorithm?
flashcards.back: An algorithm with time complexity O(2^n), O(n!), or O(n^k) where k is not fixed. These become impractical very quickly as n grows.
The Cook-Levin theorem (1971) proved that SAT is NP-complete, meaning any NP problem can be reduced to SAT in polynomial time.

flashcards.front: What is meant by 'certificate' or 'witness' in NP problems?
flashcards.back: A certificate is a piece of information that, when combined with the input, allows a polynomial-time verifier to confirm that the answer to an NP problem is 'yes'.

flashcards.front: What is the halting problem and why is it important?
flashcards.back: The halting problem asks whether a given program will halt on a given input. It is undecidable (not in P or NP) and demonstrates there are problems that cannot be solved by any algorithm.

flashcards.front: What is meant by pseudopolynomial time?
flashcards.back: Pseudopolynomial time means the runtime is polynomial in the numeric value of the input (not the length), e.g., dynamic programming for knapsack with O(nW) time.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between P, NP, and NP-complete?
flashcards.back: P ⊆ NP (all polynomial-time solvable problems are verifiable in polynomial time). NP-complete problems are the hardest in NP. If any NP-complete problem is in P, then P = NP.
